title: Keynote Address at the Boao Forum for Asia Annual Conference 2023
occasion: Boao Forum for Asia Annual Conference 2023

paragraphs.text: Distinguished guests, ladies and gentlemen, good morning. It is my great pleasure to join you here at the Boao Forum to discuss the economic outlook for Asia and the role of monetary and financial policy in supporting stable and sustainable growth. The global economy faces a complex environment, and the decisions we make in the coming period will have lasting consequences for prosperity across the region.

China's economy has entered a new phase of recovery following the optimization of pandemic control measures. The reopening process is generating renewed momentum in consumption, services, and domestic demand more broadly. In the first quarter of this year, we have observed encouraging signs in retail sales, travel, and dining — sectors that were most affected during the pandemic period.

The People's Bank of China has maintained a prudent monetary policy throughout the pandemic and into the recovery. Unlike many advanced economy central banks that undertook aggressive quantitative easing followed by sharp tightening cycles, we kept our policy rate adjustments moderate and our balance sheet expansion restrained. This approach has given us policy space that others now lack, and it has kept inflation in China well within our target range.

Our consumer price index remained around two percent in 2022, and core inflation has been even lower. This stands in contrast to the elevated inflation rates seen in the United States, Europe, and many other economies. The difference reflects both structural factors in the Chinese economy and the measured approach of our monetary policy. We will continue to prioritize price stability as a foundation for sustained economic growth.

At the same time, we recognize that certain sectors of the economy require targeted support. The property market, which has undergone significant adjustment over the past two years, remains a key area of policy focus. We have taken a series of measures to support reasonable housing demand, ease financing conditions for developers meeting prudential standards, and ensure the delivery of pre-sold housing projects. These measures are showing initial results, and we will continue to calibrate our approach as conditions evolve.

Financial stability is a prerequisite for sustainable development, and the PBOC has strengthened its macroprudential framework considerably in recent years. We have established a comprehensive stress testing regime for systemically important financial institutions, enhanced our monitoring of shadow banking activities, and developed new tools for managing systemic risk. The Chinese banking system remains well-capitalized, with a capital adequacy ratio well above regulatory minimums.

Let me now turn to the international dimension of China's financial policy. The internationalization of the renminbi has progressed steadily and organically. The RMB's share in global payments has risen, and its inclusion in the IMF's Special Drawing Rights basket reflects its growing role in the international monetary system. We have established bilateral currency swap agreements with over forty central banks, and the Cross-Border Interbank Payment System now connects participants in over one hundred countries.

I want to emphasize that RMB internationalization is driven by market demand, not by administrative directives. As China's trade and investment flows grow, as our capital markets deepen, and as foreign investors seek diversification, the natural demand for RMB-denominated assets and transactions increases. Our role as policymakers is to remove barriers and improve infrastructure to facilitate this market-driven process.

The opening of China's financial markets continues to advance. Foreign institutional investors now have access to a bond market exceeding one hundred forty trillion yuan and an equity market that is among the largest in the world. We have streamlined the QFII and RQFII programs, expanded the Bond Connect and Stock Connect channels, and introduced Swap Connect. These initiatives reflect our commitment to building a financial system that is open, transparent, and well-regulated.

Green finance is another area where China is playing a leading role. The PBOC was among the first central banks in the world to establish a green bond framework. Our green loan balance now exceeds twenty-two trillion yuan, making it the largest in the world. We have developed a green finance taxonomy, supported the establishment of carbon trading markets, and introduced carbon reduction support tools that provide low-cost funding to financial institutions for qualifying green projects.

The transition to a low-carbon economy presents both challenges and opportunities for monetary policy. Central banks must integrate climate-related financial risks into their supervisory frameworks while supporting the orderly transition of high-carbon industries. The PBOC has been working with the Network for Greening the Financial System and with fellow central banks across Asia to develop common approaches to climate risk assessment and disclosure.

Digital finance and financial technology represent another frontier of innovation. China's experience with mobile payments and digital financial services has expanded financial inclusion dramatically. Over one billion adults now use mobile payment platforms for everyday transactions. The digital RMB pilot, which is being tested in multiple cities across the country, represents the next evolution of this digital financial infrastructure.

The e-CNY is designed to complement existing payment systems, not to replace them. It preserves the two-tier structure of our monetary system, with the central bank issuing digital currency to commercial banks, which in turn distribute it to the public. Privacy protection, interoperability, and financial inclusion are core design principles. We are proceeding carefully and learning from the pilot programs before any decision on broader rollout.

For the Asian region as a whole, financial cooperation has never been more important. The Chiang Mai Initiative Multilateralization, the Asian Bond Markets Initiative, and the ASEAN+3 Macroeconomic Research Office provide a regional financial safety net that complements the global architecture. We should continue to strengthen these mechanisms and explore new areas of cooperation, including in digital payments, climate finance, and cross-border regulatory coordination.

The global monetary policy environment presents particular challenges for emerging and developing economies in Asia. As advanced economy central banks maintain elevated interest rates, capital flow volatility and exchange rate pressures can complicate macroeconomic management in smaller economies. Regional cooperation, including through central bank swap lines and coordinated communication, can help mitigate these spillover effects.

Looking ahead, the Chinese economy faces several structural challenges that monetary and financial policy must address. Population aging, the transition from investment-led to consumption-led growth, the need to climb the technology value chain, and the imperative of environmental sustainability all require careful policy calibration. The PBOC will continue to support these structural adjustments through targeted credit policies, financial reform, and prudent macroeconomic management.

I am confident in the resilience and dynamism of the Chinese economy and the broader Asian region. Asia accounts for a growing share of global output, trade, and innovation. The fundamentals that have driven decades of Asian growth — high savings rates, investment in education and infrastructure, openness to trade and technology — remain intact. Our task as policymakers is to create the conditions for these fundamentals to continue delivering inclusive prosperity.

In conclusion, the PBOC will continue to implement a prudent monetary policy that is flexible, targeted, and forward-looking. We will deepen financial opening, advance RMB internationalization through market mechanisms, promote green and digital finance, and strengthen our macroprudential framework. Through cooperation with our partners in Asia and around the world, we will work to build a more resilient, inclusive, and sustainable global financial system. Thank you.
